9	# Feature: Task Execution
10	
11	**ID:** FEAT-010
12	**Status:** In Progress
13	**Priority:** P1
14	**Owner:** DDx Team
15	
16	## Overview
17	
18	DDx owns the task execution lifecycle. The lifecycle is made of concrete layers
19	with stable public names: `run`, `try`, and `work`. Each higher layer composes
20	the layer beneath it. There are no other run kinds beyond those named layers.
21	
22	| Layer | CLI | Inputs | Outputs / side effects | Owns |
23	|---|---|---|---|---|
24	| 1 | `ddx run` | prompt + execution config | structured output, side effects, run metadata (tokens, model, duration, exit) | invocation atom; consumes upstream Fizeau execution contract |
25	| 2 | `ddx try <bead>` | bead id, base revision | new worktree state + run evidence; merge or preserve | worktree start/end capture; bead → prompt resolution; side-effect bundling |
26	| 3 | `ddx work` | bead queue, stop conditions | sequence of `ddx try` records + loop-level record (drained / blocked / deferred) | mechanical queue drain; no-progress detection |
27	
28	`ddx try` wraps `ddx run`. `ddx work` iterates `ddx try`. One on-disk
29	substrate; layer metadata distinguishes records.
30	
31	Implementation code for this boundary should follow the task-execution
32	vocabulary (`taskexec` for core layer logic, `fizeauadapter` for the service
33	bridge) rather than the legacy `internal/agent` package name. Those package
34	names should make the DDx-owned workflow boundary explicit.
35	
36	`ddx artifact regenerate <id>` is sugar over layer 1 (or layer 2 when the
37	generator edits the repo) with `produces_artifact: <id>` metadata. It is
38	not a fourth layer — it is a labeled invocation at an existing layer.
39	
40	## Agent Interaction Modes
41	
42	DDx defines four named agent interaction modes (`DDX_MODE`) to separate
43	broad interactive queue stewardship from worker execution. The mode governs
44	what an agent opened in a DDx project is permitted to do.
45	
46	| DDX_MODE | Description | Allowed actions | Prohibited actions |
47	|---|---|---|---|
48	| `queue_steward` | Default interactive mode. Surveys, triages, and advises on the queue without claiming or executing beads. | Read tracker and docs; report status; advise on bead quality; suggest readiness fixes; run `ddx bead list/ready/status/show`. | Claiming beads; creating isolated worktrees; starting `ddx work` or `ddx try` without an explicit user directive. |
49	| `bead_execution` | Worker mode. Executes one or more beads from the queue in isolated worktrees. | Full FEAT-010 layer-2 / layer-3 lifecycle: worktree creation, bead attempt, merge or preserve, evidence bundle. | Scope creep outside the named bead; switching to direct implementation without a bead. |
50	| `direct_user_implementation` | Human-directed implementation. The user has explicitly asked the agent to edit code or docs, bypassing the bead queue. | Edit code and docs as instructed; commit to the current branch. | Starting autonomous queue drain; claiming beads autonomously. |
51	| `review` | Read-only review. Grades an existing implementation against AC or specs. | Read code, docs, and evidence; return a structured verdict with per-AC findings. | Writing commits; claiming beads; modifying the tracker. |
52	
53	### Precedence
54	
55	`bead_execution` supersedes `queue_steward` when DDx invokes a worker
56	explicitly (via `ddx work`, `ddx try <id>`, or `ddx agent execute-bead`).
57	`direct_user_implementation` supersedes `queue_steward` when the user
58	explicitly asks for code or doc edits unrelated to the queue. `review`
59	supersedes `queue_steward` when the user explicitly requests a graded review
60	pass.
61	
62	Tracker instructions, merge-policy, and safety rules in AGENTS.md are
63	load-bearing in every mode and are never overridden by mode selection.
64	
65	### Worker-capacity suggestion rules
66	
67	An agent in `queue_steward` mode may suggest running `ddx work` or point
68	the operator to worker-capacity settings when:
69	
70	- The queue contains execution-ready beads that have not been attempted.
71	- The queue is blocked and operator action is required.
72	- The operator asks "why isn't anything running?" or a similar capacity question.
73	
74	The agent must not autonomously start `ddx work` from `queue_steward` mode.
75	Suggesting the command or explaining the conditions is the correct action.
76	
77	## Problem Statement
78	
79	**Current situation:** DDx has accreted two independent run-storage shapes
80	— `.ddx/exec-runs/` for generic execution evidence and
81	`.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` for tracked bead-attempt bundles. Loop behavior,
82	worktree lifecycle, and bead resolution are spread across legacy command
83	surfaces without a clean layering story. There is no single place to introspect "what runs
84	happened, at what layer, with what evidence."
85	
86	**Pain points:**
87	- Two on-disk record shapes for fundamentally similar artifacts (a single
88	  agent invocation, with logs, structured result, and provenance)
89	- No first-class concept of the task execution layers (`run` / `try` /
90	  `work`), so consumers must intuit which surface to read
91	- Stop conditions for queue drain are implicit and not observable
92	- Generated-artifact provenance has no clean hook into the run record
93	- Open-ended catalog pressure: every new "run flavor" (benchmark, replay,
94	  comparison) tempts a new on-disk shape or a new top-level verb
95	
96	**Desired outcome:** A single on-disk run substrate with explicit layer
97	metadata, three top-level CLI verbs that map 1:1 onto the layers, and a
98	narrow read-only HTTP/MCP write surface limited to artifact regeneration.
99	
100	## Task Execution Layers
101	
102	### Layer 1 — `ddx run` (invocation atom)
103	
104	A layer-1 run is one AI invocation. Inputs are a prompt, requested
105	`MinPower` and optional `MaxPower`, optional operator passthrough constraints,
106	and non-routing execution config; outputs are the structured response (text or
107	bytes), any side-effects the service performed via tools, and run metadata
108	(tokens, model, actual power, duration, exit status, session pointer).
109	
110	Layer 1 calls the upstream Fizeau execution contract directly. DDx does not
111	reimplement routing or the invocation loop; it wraps one `Execute` call with
112	provenance capture.
113	
114	`ddx artifact regenerate <id>` is layer 1 when the generator returns
115	bytes/text and DDx writes the file; it is layer 2 when the generator
116	edits the repo directly (bead-less worktree).
117	
118	### Layer 2 — `ddx try <bead>` (bead attempt)
119	
120	A layer-2 run is one bead attempt in an isolated worktree. It owns:
121	
122	- Worktree creation from a base revision and worktree finalization
123	  (merge or preserve)
124	- Worktree cleanup after finalization, failed setup, failed publish, and
125	  ordinary interruption
126	- Bead → prompt resolution (description, acceptance, governing artifacts)
127	- Side-effect bundling (commits, evidence, no-changes rationale)
128	- One or more layer-1 invocations recorded inside the attempt record
129	
130	A layer-2 record references its child layer-1 records by run id.
131	
132	Layer 2 owns bead-attempt success classification. DDx determines success from
133	the artifacts it owns: commit presence, merge/preserve result, no-changes
134	rationale, post-run checks, review verdicts, and cooldown policy. The agent's
135	exit status and actual model/power are inputs to that decision, not the whole
136	decision.
137	
138	Layer 2 records raw attempt evidence. Layer 3 applies
139	[`TD-031`](../../02-design/technical-designs/TD-031-bead-state-machine.md) when
140	that evidence requires durable bead action such as close-as-already-satisfied,
141	human triage, blocked, retry cooldown, or stale no_changes metadata cleanup.
142	
143	### Quality hooks
144	
145	ADR-023 defines two lifecycle quality hooks owned by the layer-2 and layer-3
146	execution loop. They are hooks inside `ExecuteBeadLoopRuntime`, not new run
147	layers and not new bead-schema fields.
148	
149	`BeadReadinessHook` is the product concept: it runs after the worker has
150	selected a dependency-ready candidate but before DDx claims the bead or
151	creates the implementation worktree. The canonical decision being made is bead
152	readiness assessment, the pre-claim check for tractability and actionability;
153	lint/rubric scoring is the diagnostic pass inside readiness, and post-attempt
154	triage is a separate after-evidence action.
155	The implementation may still call the compatibility entrypoint
156	`PreClaimIntakeHook` and record `MODE: intake` for legacy compatibility, but
157	those names are aliases only. Within readiness, the nested bead-lifecycle
158	workflow skill performs lint/rubric scoring to support the decision, and the
159	score is diagnostic evidence rather than a separate queue action. Post-attempt
160	triage is separate and runs only after evidence exists. The hook has enough
161	context to evaluate title, description, acceptance criteria, labels, parent,
162	deps, bead type, spec-id, prior attempt history, and whether the bead is
163	atomic enough to execute. The hook invokes the nested bead-lifecycle workflow
164	skill under the `ddx` skill tree and records readiness evidence in the
165	layer-3 run record.
166	
167	`ddx work` wires this hook by default in both CLI and server-managed worker
168	paths. Decomposition decisions request Fizeau's `smart` model-ref and run with a
169	strong `MinPower` floor, defaulting to the smart/top-power tier floor when no
170	project-specific splitter override is configured. DDx does not choose the
171	concrete model; it passes the raised power floor to Fizeau and preserves any
172	operator-supplied harness/provider/model passthrough constraints. If those
173	constraints cannot satisfy the strong floor, DDx records readiness as
174	unavailable (`readiness_error` / `intake_error`) instead of treating the bead as
175	ambiguous or in need of human review; the worker then continues through the
176	normal implementation route with the same operator passthrough constraints.
177	
178	The readiness assessment result is one of:
179	
180	- `actionable_atomic` — claim and execute normally.
181	- `actionable_but_rewritten` — DDx applied a validated replacement rewrite or
182	  metadata update that makes the bead more execution-ready before claim. Safe
183	  rewrites may replace noisy, stale, duplicated, or brittle description text
184	  with a fit-for-purpose bead body instead of appending more prompt burden. They may
185	  also format the description into the authoring template, add discovered
186	  file:line evidence, add an obvious test command, or wire deterministic
187	  labels/parent/deps. The mutation is recorded through `ddx bead` paths before
188	  claim, with before/after hashes and enough evidence to audit the replacement.
189	- `too_large_decomposed` — DDx created child beads, mapped every parent AC to
190	  child ACs or an explicit `needs_human` / `non_scope` marker, blocked the
191	  parent, and did not execute the parent.
192	- `ambiguous_needs_human` — the bead/spec is unclear, contradictory,
193	  unverifiable, or missing acceptance criteria that DDx cannot safely invent.
194	  DDx blocks or marks the bead `needs_human` and does not claim it.
195	- `readiness_error` / `intake_error` — readiness infrastructure failed. In
196	  migration/WARN mode this fails open with evidence; in reliable factory/BLOCK
197	  mode it may skip the candidate for the current pass but must not park it
198	  behind a cooldown unless a retryable time-based class is recorded.
199	
200	Safe rewrites are validated against durable context, not against a vague
201	"same spirit" comparison to the prior bead text. The prior bead can itself be
202	the problem: it may contain stale line numbers, duplicated history, chat-shaped
203	noise, or contradictory wording. A safe replacement must preserve explicit
204	commitments that remain valid, especially acceptance criteria, non-scope, named
205	files/tests, dependencies, and governing artifact references; it must either
206	carry forward durable root-cause evidence or replace it with current file:line
207	evidence from the repository or governing artifact. The original text is kept in
208	readiness evidence, not copied into the replacement body. Rewrites must optimize
209	prompt fitness rather than raw length: expanding a one-line underspecified bead
210	is safe when the added durable context is required for execution, and compressing
211	a noisy bead is safe when explicit commitments remain preserved.
212	
213	Readiness must reject the rewrite and park the bead as `ambiguous_needs_human`
214	when preservation cannot be proven from durable anchors. It must not invent
215	product behavior, choose between conflicting requirements, change scope, delete
216	unresolved constraints, or guess a missing governing artifact.
217	
218	`PostAttemptTriageHook` runs after the attempt has produced its local evidence:
219	agent result, commit/no-commit state, no-changes rationale if any, post-run
220	checks, adversarial review verdict, merge/preserve result, and the readiness
221	report.
222	It uses the same bead-lifecycle skill to classify whether the outcome is a
223	normal attempt result, a quality-policy failure, a missing-evidence failure, or
224	an infrastructure failure. This is distinct from bead readiness assessment and
225	from the lint/rubric scoring used inside readiness. The hook must never rewrite
226	the attempt result or erase artifacts; it only adds triage evidence and feeds
227	retry/stop classification.
228	
229	If post-attempt triage finds that an implementation attempt stopped because the
230	bead was too large or the worker could not legally decompose inside its
231	worktree/depth context, the layer-3 worker must invoke the same orchestrator
232	decomposition path used by `BeadReadinessHook`. This is machine-actionable
233	work: DDx files child beads, records the AC map, and blocks the parent unless
234	the split is lossy, ambiguous, or at the queue-level decomposition depth cap.
235	The operator is not required merely because the implementer could not split
236	from inside the attempted execution.
237	
238	`ExecuteBeadReport` gains `OutcomeReason` beside the existing `Disrupted`
239	signal. `Disrupted` remains the mechanical indicator that normal completion was
240	interrupted. `OutcomeReason` is the stable classification string that explains
241	why the attempt ended, such as `lint_warn`, `lint_blocked`,
242	`lint_hook_error_fail_open`, `readiness_ambiguous`, `readiness_decomposed`,
243	`triage_missing_evidence`, `review_fixable_gap`, `review_spec_gap`,
244	`review_too_large`, `post_run_check_failed`, or `success`. Layer-3 `ddx work`
245	records aggregate these reasons when evaluating retry eligibility, no-progress,
246	blocked, and deferred stop conditions.
247	
248	The hook evidence lives in the attempt evidence bundle and is surfaced through
249	the unified run substrate. The originating bead may receive an evidence event
250	only for explicit operator override (`--force --reason`) or other existing
251	audit events; lint scores are not copied into `.ddx/beads.jsonl`.
252	
253	### Layer 3 — `ddx work` (queue drain)
254	
255	A layer-3 run is one drain of the bead queue. It iterates `ddx try`
256	across ready beads until a stop condition is met. It owns:
257	
258	- Queue iteration order
259	- Pre-claim readiness, safe bead improvement, decomposition, claim acquisition,
260	  claim release, and shutdown/interruption cleanup for claimed beads, using the
261	  TD-031 claim-state contract
262	- Durable bead action after each layer-2 attempt, using TD-031's outcome,
263	  no_changes, cooldown, and stale-metadata rules
264	- No-progress / stop-condition evaluation
265	- A loop-level record that references its child layer-2 records by
266	  attempt id and reports terminal disposition (drained, blocked,
267	  deferred, no-progress)
268	
269	Content-aware supervisory decisions (e.g., "comparison failed → enqueue
270	reconciliation beads") are not layer-3 concerns. Those are skill or
271	plugin compositions on top of the task execution layers.
272	
273	Layer 3 treats raw attempt evidence separately from durable bead action. When a
274	worker stops, receives SIGTERM/SIGINT, loses its child agent process, or honors
275	operator cancel before a terminal bead mutation, it follows TD-031: preserve
276	evidence, record the interruption/disruption, release the claim, and leave the
277	bead re-claimable unless an explicit retryable cooldown or blocker was recorded.
278	Shutdown is not itself a reason to park a bead on `execute-loop-retry-after`.
279	
280	## Agent Power and Retry
281	
282	DDx owns retry policy between bead attempts. If an attempt produces classified
283	evidence that more capable reasoning could plausibly change the result, DDx may
284	retry the bead with a higher `MinPower`.
285	
286	Fizeau owns routing within the requested power bounds. A retry request says
287	"use at least this much power" by raising `MinPower`; an explicit `MaxPower`, if
288	supplied, remains a hard upper bound. Operator-supplied `--harness`,
289	`--provider`, and `--model` values may be sent to Fizeau as passthrough
290	constraints, but DDx does not interpret them. Each layer-1 record stores:
291	
292	- requested `MinPower` and optional `MaxPower`
293	- requested passthrough constraints, if any
294	- actual model and actual power reported by Fizeau
295	- run outcome and DDx attempt outcome
296	
297	DDx may query Fizeau's available model/power catalog to choose escalation
298	targets such as "retry using only top-power models." DDx uses the returned power
299	numbers only to compute `MinPower` thresholds; Fizeau still chooses the
300	concrete model.
301	
302	Passthrough constraints stay sticky across retries. DDx may increase `MinPower`
303	between attempts, but it must not alter or drop operator-supplied
304	harness/provider/model passthrough values to make escalation work. If the
305	combination is invalid or constraining, the agent owns that typed error or
306	actual route. DDx stops with `blocked_by_passthrough_constraint` or
307	`agent_power_unsatisfied`, records the evidence, and reports operator action
308	required; it does not remove pins, widen pins, call `ResolveRoute`, or retry in a
309	loop.
310	
311	### Retry eligibility
312	
313	Power retry is eligible only when DDx-owned evidence shows that a stronger model
314	could plausibly help after a valid attempt started. Eligible classes include:
315	
316	- `capability_insufficient` — the agent attempted the bead, but reasoning
317	  quality or implementation quality was insufficient.
318	- `post_run_check_failed` — tests or gates failed after a valid checkout and
319	  attempted change.
320	- `review_fixable_gap` — adversarial review found implementation or test gaps
321	  plausibly addressable by another automated cycle.
322	- `no_changes_after_attempt` — the agent had a valid checkout and task context
323	  but produced no usable change, subject to the no-progress budget.
324	
325	Power retry is not eligible for deterministic setup or operator-action
326	failures:
327	
328	- dirty worktree, merge/land conflict, missing checkout, invalid bead metadata,
329	  unresolved dependencies, config parse errors, missing harness binaries,
330	  authentication failures, command-not-found/toolchain setup failures
331	- readiness ambiguity, missing acceptance criteria, spec contradictions,
332	  decomposition overflow, review errors, reviewer context overflow, claim races,
333	  routing preflight rejection, quota/transport disruption, and any other class
334	  where the implementer did not receive valid task context and an opportunity to
335	  act
336	- `blocked_by_passthrough_constraint` / `agent_power_unsatisfied`
337	- any status where retry would require DDx to inspect, remove, rewrite, or
338	  substitute `--harness`, `--provider`, or `--model`
339	
340	Every retry/stop decision records the classification and evidence used. The
341	policy may inspect DDx attempt outcomes and the agent's typed status; it must
342	not branch on concrete provider/model identity.
343	
344	ADR-024 is the decision record for this policy. FEAT-010 owns when `ddx try` or
345	`ddx work` schedules a retry, how no-progress and stop conditions are recorded,
346	how review-error retry scopes are counted, and how budget caps stop a drain.
347	FEAT-006 owns only the agent request envelope used by those decisions.
348	
349	### Execution Cycle Traceability
350	
351	A bead execution is an append-only chain of implementation/review cycles. Layer
352	3 owns the chain; layer 2 owns the evidence for each cycle. A repair attempt
353	must create a new cycle record rather than overwrite the rejected one.
354	
355	Each cycle records:
356	
357	- `cycle_index`
358	- `attempt_id`
359	- `base_rev`
360	- `result_rev` when a stable candidate exists
361	- implementer layer-1 run ids, requested power bounds, actual route/power, and
362	  cost/usage facts
363	- verification command output and exit status
364	- `review_group_id`, reviewer layer-1 run ids, reviewer requested/actual
365	  route facts, aggregate verdict, per-AC evidence summary, and reviewer cost
366	- prior review linkage (`repair_context_from_review_group`) when this cycle is
367	  a repair attempt
368	- final cycle decision: close, retry, decompose, block, manual-required, or
369	  budget stop
370	
371	When a review requires another implementation cycle, the next implementation
372	prompt includes the prior review group's findings as required repair context.
373	The prior cycle remains immutable so operators can answer what changed, why the
374	first result was rejected, and why the final result passed.
375	
376	### Candidate-Cycle Pipeline
377	
378	A **candidate** is the stable commit produced by one implementation pass and
379	pinned as the subject of the review gate before land. A candidate exists when
380	the implementation produces at least one commit and post-run checks have been
381	attempted.
382	
383	A **candidate ref** is a project-root git ref
384	(`refs/ddx/iterations/<attempt-id>/<cycle-index>`) that pins the candidate
385	commit so reviewers can inspect a stable revision even after worktree cleanup.
386	
387	A **cycle** is one implementation pass plus its associated review gate: one
388	implementation pass that produces a candidate, the read-only tool reviewer gate
389	run against that candidate in the still-live worktree, and the resulting
390	disposition (land, repair, or stop).
391	
392	A **repair cycle** is a cycle triggered when the prior cycle's review verdict is
393	`review_fixable_gap`. The repair implementation runs in the same still-live
394	attempt worktree and is **append-only** — no `git reset`, `git commit --amend`,
395	`git squash`, or `git rebase` against the prior candidate is permitted. The
396	repair prompt includes the prior review group's findings as required context.
397	
398	A **read-only tool reviewer** is a reviewer agent that runs in the still-live
399	attempt worktree with access to read-only filesystem tools (file reads,
400	searches) but no write tools. The bounded evidence bundle assembled per FEAT-022
401	is the canonical review input; same-worktree read-only tool access is
402	supplemental and does not override the bounded bundle. The reviewer cannot
403	create commits or modify worktree state.
404	
405	**Terminal dispositions** for a candidate-cycle attempt:
406	- `landed` — candidate was approved and merged to the base branch.
407	- `preserved` — candidate is approved but land was deferred; worktree retained.
408	- `parked` — repair budget exhausted without approval; bead moved to `needs_human`.
409	- `conflicted` — approved candidate failed to land due to merge conflict; bead
410	  returned to `open` for re-attempt with a fresh `base_rev`.
411	- `budget-stopped` — drain-level cost or no-progress budget tripped; worktree
412	  preserved with evidence.
413	
414	**Candidate-cycle sequence** (in the still-live attempt worktree, before land):
415	
416	1. Create isolated worktree from `base_rev`.
417	2. Run implementation pass; collect `base_rev`, `result_rev`, run ids,
418	   verification output, and cost facts.
419	3. Pin candidate ref; emit `candidate-pinned`.
420	4. Run candidate checks (post-run tests, lint, gates). Failure emits
421	   `candidate-checks-failed`; plausibly capability-sensitive failures are
422	   eligible for a repair cycle when `repair_max_cycles` allows.
423	5. Dispatch read-only tool reviewers in the still-live worktree, **before land**.
424	   Record `review_group_id`, reviewer run ids, per-AC verdict, and cost.
425	6. On unanimous `APPROVE`: land, emit `final-result-landed`, close bead, clean up.
426	7. On `REQUEST_CHANGES` / `BLOCK` classified `review_fixable_gap` within
427	   `repair_max_cycles`: emit `repair-cycle-started`, run append-only repair in
428	   same worktree, return to step 3.
429	8. On `repair_max_cycles` exhausted: emit `repair-cycle-exhausted`, preserve
430	   worktree, park bead in `needs_human`.
431	9. On approved candidate that fails to land (merge conflict): emit
432	   `approved-land-conflict`, release claim, return bead to `open`.
433	
434	**Budget axes** (each is distinct from cost, rate-limit/provider, and
435	no-progress budgets):
436	
437	- **`repair_max_cycles`** — maximum repair cycles per attempt. Exhaustion fires
438	  `repair-cycle-exhausted` and parks the bead in `needs_human`; it does not
439	  consume the no-progress counter.
440	- **`review_max_retries_per_candidate`** — maximum reviewer retry attempts for a
441	  single candidate ref when reviewer invocations fail. Exhaustion fires
442	  `review-manual-required`. A new candidate ref (next repair cycle or fresh
443	  `ddx try`) resets this counter independently; it is strictly per-candidate.
444	
445	These axes do not interact with the drain-level cost cap (FEAT-014), the
446	rate-limit retry budget internal to a single attempt, or the no-progress counter
447	tracking consecutive attempts with no commit.
448	
449	**Transitional single-slot allowance.** Until the two-slot quorum reviewer bead
450	lands, a single reviewer slot satisfies the review gate. The removal condition
451	is the landing of the bead that implements two-slot quorum aggregation.
452	
453	### Escalation, fallback, retry, and review decision tree
454	
455	The layer-3 drain evaluates each ready bead through this mechanical sequence:
456	
457	1. **Eligibility and readiness.** Pick a dependency-ready candidate. Run the
458	   readiness gate. Validated replacement rewrites happen before claim and should
459	   leave a clearer implementation prompt, expanding underspecified beads or
460	   compressing noisy/stale beads as the durable context requires; original text
461	   is preserved in readiness evidence for audit. Too-large work is decomposed
462	   before an implementation attempt. Ambiguous or underspecified work is blocked
463	   with `needs_human`. Readiness infrastructure failure records evidence and
464	   follows the configured fail-open/factory-mode policy; it never creates an
465	   unexplained cooldown.
466	2. **Claim.** Claim only an `actionable_atomic` or safely rewritten bead. Claim
467	   races skip the bead for the current pass without cooldown.
468	3. **Primary implementation cycle.** Run one layer-2 implementation attempt.
469	   A candidate result records `base_rev`, `result_rev`, implementation run ids,
470	   route/power facts, verification output, and cost. Already-satisfied
471	   no-changes may close only after the required verification evidence exists;
472	   otherwise no-changes outcomes follow TD-031.
473	4. **Adversarial pre-close review.** Review is enabled by default. For every
474	   close-eligible `result_rev`, layer 2 pins a candidate ref and dispatches two
475	   read-only tool reviewer runs **in the still-live attempt worktree, before
476	   land**, each carrying `role=reviewer`, a stronger `MinPower` floor than the
477	   implementer, and correlation metadata (`review_group_id`, `result_rev`,
478	   reviewer slot, and implementer route facts). The bounded evidence bundle is
479	   the canonical review input; same-worktree read-only tool access is
480	   supplemental. Close is permitted only when the aggregate review is unanimous
481	   `APPROVE` with per-AC evidence.
482	5. **Review classification.** Any evidenced `REQUEST_CHANGES` or `BLOCK`
483	   prevents close. `review_fixable_gap` schedules a repair cycle on the same
484	   bead when `repair_max_cycles` allows, injecting the review findings as
485	   required repair context and optionally raising `MinPower`; it preserves the
486	   retry path and does not park the bead in the `needs_human` lane.
487	   `review_spec_gap`, `review_missing_acceptance`, `review_too_large`, and
488	   non-mechanical unsafe or out-of-scope findings park the bead in the
489	   `needs_human` operator-attention lane, clear the active claim, and do not ask
490	   another implementer to guess. Malformed, empty, context-overflow, and
491	   transport reviewer failures emit `review-error` scoped to `result_rev` and
492	   reviewer slot; after `review_max_retries_per_candidate` they emit
493	   `review-manual-required`, clear the active claim, park the bead in the
494	   `needs_human` lane, and do not close. The `review_fixable_gap` path stays on
495	   the automatic retry track instead of converting into operator review.
496	6. **Infrastructure fallback.** Transport, quota, rate-limit, command setup,
497	   context cancellation, routing preflight rejection, and worker disruption are
498	   not model-capability failures. They emit structured evidence and either stay
499	   immediately retryable or use a bounded retry-after when the same class is
500	   time-based. HTTP 429 retry happens inside one attempt with `rate-limit-retry`
501	   events.
502	7. **Capability retry.** Failed checks, `review_fixable_gap`,
503	   capability-insufficient attempts, and eligible no-changes-after-attempt may
504	   schedule a higher-power retry by raising the next request's `MinPower`.
505	   Passthrough constraints remain unchanged. If the requested power cannot be
506	   satisfied under those constraints, DDx records a terminal operator-action
507	   classification and stops retrying that bead.
508	8. **Cost stop.** Implementation and review attempts both contribute reported
509	   billable cost to the drain budget according to FEAT-014 cost-class metadata.
510	   When the cap trips, the drain records a budget stop and stops claiming new
511	   work; the cap is not reported as model failure.
512	
513	## Substrate Unification
514	
515	### One record shape
516	
517	All task execution layers persist to a single on-disk shape under
518	`.ddx/runs/<run-id>/`. The record has the following common fields and a
519	discriminating `layer` field; layer-specific extensions live in named
520	subobjects rather than separate file trees.
521	
522	Common fields:
523	- `run_id` — stable, sortable identifier
524	- `layer` — `1` | `2` | `3`
525	- `parent_run_id` — pointer to the enclosing layer's run, if any
526	- `started_at` / `finished_at`
527	- `terminal_status` — `success` | `failure` | `timeout` | `error` |
528	  `no_progress` (layer 3 only) | `preserved` (layer 2 only)
529	- `actor`, `host`, `git_revision`, `ddx_version`
530	- `produces_artifact` — optional artifact id when this run regenerates
531	  an artifact
532	- `attachments[]` — references to large bodies (logs, structured payloads,
533	  bundles) stored alongside the record rather than inline
534	
535	Layer extensions:
536	- `layer1` — prompt reference, agent config, model id, token usage,
537	  upstream session id, structured response pointer
538	- `layer2` — bead id, base revision, worktree path, finalization mode
539	  (`merge` | `preserve`), child layer-1 run ids, evidence-bundle pointer
540	- `layer3` — queue snapshot pointer, stop-condition evaluation log,
541	  child layer-2 attempt ids, drain disposition
542	
543	Records are append-only. A new run never rewrites a previous one.
544	
545	### On-disk migration
546	
547	The two pre-existing trees collapse into the unified substrate:
548	
549	- `.ddx/exec-runs/<run-id>/` → `.ddx/runs/<run-id>/` with `layer: 1`
550	- `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` → `.ddx/runs/<run-id>/` with `layer: 2`
551	  and the existing prompt / manifest / result / checks / provenance
552	  surfaced as layer-2 evidence-bundle attachments
553	
554	Layer-3 records are new (no pre-existing tree).
555	
556	Migration is one-way and read-compatible:
557	- Writers always target `.ddx/runs/`
558	- Readers tolerate legacy paths during a documented migration window
559	  and surface them through the unified read API as if they were layer-1
560	  or layer-2 records, then a one-shot rewrite normalizes them on disk
561	- After the migration window closes, legacy paths are removed; readers
562	  no longer fall back
563	
564	The migration window, the rewrite tool, and the cutoff signal are
565	specified in the implementation epic, not here.
566	
567	## `ddx work` Stop Conditions
568	
569	`ddx work` drains the queue until **any** of the following stop
570	conditions is met. The terminal disposition of the layer-3 record names
571	which condition fired.
572	
573	1. **`drained`** — queue is empty (no ready beads remain).
574	2. **`blocked`** — every remaining ready bead has produced a terminal
575	   non-success outcome on its most recent attempt and re-attempt is not
576	   permitted under the current policy.
577	3. **`deferred`** — a configured wall-clock or attempt-count budget is
578	   exhausted. The remaining queue is intact and a subsequent `ddx work`
579	   resumes from where this one stopped.
580	4. **`no_progress`** — `N` consecutive valid implementation attempts produced
581	   no commit and no merged side-effect on the base branch (default `N = 3`;
582	   configurable). A valid implementation attempt is one where DDx created a
583	   checkout, supplied task context to the implementer, and the implementer had
584	   an opportunity to act. Intake blocks, decomposition, claim races, routing
585	   preflight rejection, quota/transport/auth/tool setup failures, review errors,
586	   and operator-action classes do not increment this counter.
587	5. **`signal`** — a SIGINT/SIGTERM was received. On the first signal, DDx
588	   prints `Cancel received, shutting down gracefully`, cancels the active
589	   command context, stops claiming new beads, and gives the in-flight
590	   `ddx try` (if any) one cooperative cleanup path to preserve evidence,
591	   release its claim, and leave the bead re-claimable. A second signal may
592	   hard-abort the process.
593	6. **`resource_exhausted`** — DDx cannot safely create or publish execution
594	   state because a required execution root is out of bytes, out of inodes, not
595	   writable, or cannot register/remove git worktrees. Resource exhaustion is
596	   host infrastructure failure, not bead failure and not model no-progress.
597	   `ddx work` stops claiming new beads after recording the failed `ddx try`
598	   setup evidence and a cleanup summary. It must not continue to the next
599	   ready bead in the same drain.
600	
601	Stop-condition evaluation normally runs **between** `ddx try` invocations.
602	`signal` is the exception: the first signal cancels an in-flight attempt
603	cooperatively rather than waiting for the model to finish. The cancelled
604	attempt is mechanically disrupted, not a model no-progress failure, and ordinary
605	interrupt shutdown MUST NOT park the bead behind `execute-loop-retry-after`.
606	
607	### Execution Resource Cleanup
608	
609	DDx-created execution resources must have an owner, a liveness signal, a
610	retention policy, and a cleanup path. Missing ownership, missing liveness, or
611	unbounded accumulation is a correctness bug in the execution substrate.
612	
613	Layer 2 owns inline cleanup for one `ddx try` attempt. Before claim or worktree
614	creation it validates the required execution roots:
615	
616	- the temporary worktree root, currently `$TMPDIR/ddx-exec-wt` unless
617	  overridden
618	- the durable evidence root, currently `.ddx/executions` during migration and
619	  `.ddx/runs` for new substrate records
620	- git worktree registration/removal for the project repository
621	
622	The validation checks writability, free bytes, and free inodes where the
623	platform exposes inode counts. If validation fails, `ddx try` runs one
624	immediate DDx-scoped cleanup pass, re-checks, and then either proceeds or
625	returns `resource_exhausted` without claiming the bead.
626	
627	`ddx work` and server-managed workers use the same cleanup manager before the
628	first claim and before later claims whenever any checked temp or evidence root
629	falls below the soft cleanup trigger of **512 MiB free bytes** or **8192 free
630	inodes**. If cleanup does not restore all roots above the hard stop floor of
631	**64 MiB free bytes** and **1024 free inodes**, the loop stops visibly with
632	`resource_exhausted` and claims no more beads.
633	
634	After an attempt starts, Layer 2 removes the isolated worktree when the result
635	has been merged, explicitly preserved, classified as no-changes/no-evidence, or
636	interrupted through the cooperative shutdown path. Failed setup must remove any
637	partial unregistered directory it created. A worktree may remain only when DDx
638	records an explicit preserve decision with evidence pointing at the retained
639	path or ref.
640	
641	DDx-owned cleanup scope includes execution worktrees, DDx-created test and e2e
642	scratch roots, generated test binaries, and run-state or liveness files. The
643	cleanup manager may delete only DDx-owned paths. Recognized DDx-owned scratch
644	prefixes are: `ddx-exec-wt`, `ddx-claim-heartbeats`, `ddx-metric-keepalive`,
645	`ddx-test-`, and `ddx-e2e-`. Any temp directory that contains a `cleanup.json`
646	metadata file with matching project ownership is also DDx-owned regardless of
647	its name.
648	
649	Deletion is permitted only when all of the following hold:
650	
651	- **Metadata-backed paths**: the `cleanup.json` liveness record is expired or
652	  the owning attempt has reached a terminal state.
653	- **Metadata-less recognized-prefix paths**: the directory's mtime is at least
654	  **6 hours** old and no live PID or active session is attached.
655	
656	The manager must preserve published evidence, registered active worktrees, and
657	anything outside DDx-owned roots.
658	
659	Layer 3 owns loop cleanup. `ddx work` runs cleanup:
660	
661	- once at startup, before the first queue claim
662	- before the next claim after any setup/finalization failure
663	- periodically while a long-lived poll worker remains active
664	- during graceful signal shutdown before exit
665	
666	Long-lived DDx processes also start a background cleanup worker. The background
667	worker runs occasionally with jitter and a project-level cleanup lock so multiple
668	workers do not all prune at once. It is conservative and DDx-scoped: it may
669	remove stale unregistered directories under DDx temp roots, registered DDx
670	worktrees whose attempt is terminal or whose liveness marker is stale, stale
671	heartbeat/liveness files for dead PIDs, and partial setup directories that were
672	never published as complete evidence. It must not remove preserved worktrees,
673	`refs/ddx/iterations/...`, complete `.ddx/runs/<id>` or
674	`.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>` evidence, active worktrees with live
675	PID/session liveness, or non-DDx directories.
676	
677	Cleanup is observable but not noisy. Routine passes are trace/debug or worker
678	events. Passes that reclaim significant bytes or inodes emit an operator-visible
679	summary such as `cleanup: removed 37 stale ddx worktrees, freed 14210 inodes`.
680	Resource exhaustion after cleanup is a hard visible stop message and a layer-3
681	`resource_exhausted` disposition.
682	
683	Cleanup reporting includes scratch roots removed, bytes and inodes reclaimed,
684	preserved paths, and blocked warnings so operators can see why cleanup stopped
685	short.
686	
687	### Long-running default (`--poll-interval`)
688	
689	Per ddx-dc157075, the default `--poll-interval` for `ddx work` is **30s**.
690	With a positive poll interval the
691	worker stays alive across empty polls (`drained` becomes a transient
692	"running (idle)" substate, not a terminal exit). The loop exits only on
693	the conditions enumerated above plus `signal`, fatal config errors, or
694	the explicit operator opt-outs:
695	
696	- `--once` — process at most one ready bead, then exit.
697	- `--poll-interval=0` — legacy "drain-and-exit" semantics: when the
698	  queue empties, return immediately without polling.
699	
700	Server-managed workers spawned via `POST /api/workers/execute-loop`
701	inherit the same 30s default when the request omits `poll_interval`. The
702	worker record exposes a `substate` field set to `"idle"` while the loop
703	is sleeping between empty polls; it is cleared as soon as a candidate is
704	picked. Terminal worker states (`exited`, `failed`, `stopped`, `reaped`)
705	always clear `substate`.
706	
707	The structured `loop.end` event carries an `exit_reason` field
708	(`once_complete`, `explicit_poll_zero`, `sigint`, `sigterm`,
709	`fatal_config`, `resource_exhausted`; `providers_exhausted` is reserved for the
710	quota-pause work in ddx-aede917d).
711	
712	Power escalation is evaluated between attempts as part of retry policy. A
713	higher-power retry raises `MinPower` and resets neither the evidence history nor
714	the no-progress counter; it is recorded as the next `ddx try` with its own
715	requested bounds and actual power metadata.
716	
717	The evaluation log is persisted on the layer-3 record so a human or
718	tool can audit which condition fired and on which iteration.
719	
720	## Quality Hooks
721	
722	ADR-023 (`../../02-design/adr/ADR-023-bead-lifecycle-quality-policy.md`)
723	adds two quality hooks to the layer-2/layer-3 lifecycle. The hooks are
724	implemented in `ExecuteBeadLoopRuntime` at the same boundary that already
725	owns bead selection, attempt finalization, and retry classification.
726	
727	`BeadReadinessHook` runs after a bead has been selected and verified as
728	dependency-eligible, but before DDx claims it or creates the implementation
729	worktree. It performs the canonical bead readiness assessment, the pre-claim
730	decision about tractability and actionability; lint/rubric scoring is the
731	diagnostic pass inside readiness, and post-attempt triage is a separate
732	after-evidence action. The legacy compatibility name `PreClaimIntakeHook` and
733	`MODE: intake` may still appear in migration code or notes, but they are not
734	the product concept.
735	
736	The hook receives the bead record, current execution policy, hook mode
737	(`WARN-ONLY` or `BLOCK`), and the layer-3 evidence directory. It invokes the
738	bead-lifecycle workflow skill from FEAT-011 and writes a bead readiness,
739	scope, and decomposition report. In WARN-ONLY mode, the report is diagnostic
740	unless the hook can safely improve or decompose the bead. In BLOCK/factory
741	mode, a valid low readiness score, unsafe ambiguity, or too-large
742	classification stops implementation before claim.
743	
744	`PostAttemptTriageHook` runs after the attempt has produced its owned
745	evidence: commits or no-changes rationale, command results, review verdicts,
746	merge/preserve result, and any structural validation result. It invokes the
747	same bead-lifecycle workflow skill against the attempt bundle and returns an
748	attempt classification such as prompt-quality issue, task-quality issue,
749	infrastructure failure, deterministic setup failure, or operator action
750	required. This classification feeds retry reporting and operator UX; it does
751	not replace the existing success/no-changes/failed outcome taxonomy.
752	
753	Both hooks are fail-open for infrastructure failures. If the skill package is
754	missing, the hook process crashes, evidence cannot be written, or the model
755	invocation fails, DDx records the hook error and continues with the underlying
756	attempt flow in WARN-ONLY mode. In BLOCK/factory mode, hook infrastructure
757	failure may skip the current candidate for the pass with observable evidence,
758	but it must not park the bead behind an unexplained cooldown. Only a
759	successfully computed readiness/lint result can block dispatch as an authoring
760	or scope failure. `--force --reason <text>` records an event with the actor,
761	reason, hook mode, and overridden criteria before proceeding.
762	
763	Long-running entrypoints perform a lightweight DDx runtime preflight before
764	starting work. This is not a full `ddx doctor` run: it avoids network checks,
765	package-manager checks, route probing, stale-document scans, and other broad
766	diagnostics. The preflight is limited to conditions that can predictably degrade
767	the command about to run:
768	
769	- project root resolution;
770	- `.ddx/config.yaml` parseability;
771	- `.ddx/beads.jsonl` parseability for bead-execution commands;
772	- the root `ddx` project skill under `.agents/skills/ddx/SKILL.md` or
773	  `.claude/skills/ddx/SKILL.md`;
774	- the nested `bead-lifecycle` skill when readiness/lint/triage hooks are wired;
775	- legacy project-local skill symlinks from the pre-FEAT-015 install model.
776	
777	Passing preflight checks are silent. Failed checks are observable and
778	actionable. `ddx work`, `ddx try`, and compatibility `ddx agent execute-loop`
779	fail on missing required project/tracker state, but warn once per process and
780	continue for optional lifecycle-hook degradation in WARN-ONLY mode. `ddx server`
781	fails on missing required server project state, but missing lifecycle skills or
782	legacy skill layout are surfaced as degraded startup diagnostics and health
783	metadata rather than blocking the HTTP server. When the lifecycle skill is
784	missing, both startup preflight and hook-time errors must include the project
785	root, the exact checked paths, and remediation:
786	
787	```text
788	Run:
789	  ddx update --force
790	  ddx doctor
791	```
792	
793	`ExecuteBeadReport` gains an `OutcomeReason` field beside the existing
794	`Disrupted` flag. `Disrupted` remains the coarse boolean that the attempt did
795	not complete normally. `OutcomeReason` is the machine-readable reason selected
796	by lifecycle classification, for example:
797	
798	- `bead_lint_warn`
799	- `bead_lint_blocked`
800	- `readiness_actionable_atomic`
801	- `readiness_rewritten`
802	- `readiness_decomposed`
803	- `readiness_needs_human`
804	- `forced_with_reason`
805	- `triage_prompt_quality`
806	- `triage_task_quality`
807	- `triage_infrastructure_failure`
808	- `triage_operator_action`
809	- `hook_unavailable_fail_open`
810	
811	Layer-3 `ddx work` records include the hook summaries for each child layer-2
812	attempt in the stop-condition evaluation log. A blocked lint result counts as a
813	terminal non-success outcome for that bead under the current policy, so the
814	existing `blocked`, `deferred`, and `no_progress` loop rules can reason about
815	it without a fourth run layer or a separate run type.
816	
817	## No Run-Type Catalog
818	
819	DDx will not introduce additional run kinds beyond `run`, `try`, and `work`.
820	Comparison, replay, benchmark, adversarial review, effort estimate,
821	bead breakdown, and similar workflows are **skill compositions** over
822	the task execution layers — they emit ordinary layer-1, layer-2, or layer-3
823	records, optionally tagged with a free-form `skill` label, and do not get
824	bespoke storage shapes, bespoke verbs, or bespoke spec sections.
825	
826	This is a load-bearing non-goal: every future "we should add a fourth
827	layer for X" proposal is rejected by reference to this section. If a
828	new workflow cannot be expressed as a composition over `run` / `try` /
829	`work`, the gap is in the task execution contract, not in the catalog.
830	
831	## Requirements
832	
833	### Functional
834	
835	1. **One on-disk substrate** — all run records, regardless of layer,
836	   persist under `.ddx/runs/<run-id>/` with the common record shape and
837	   a discriminating `layer` field.
838	2. **Layer metadata** — every run record carries `layer`,
839	   `parent_run_id`, and the layer-appropriate extension subobject.
840	3. **Layer-1 invocation** — `ddx run` produces exactly one layer-1
841	   record per invocation; consumes upstream Fizeau execution contract
842	   per CONTRACT-003.
843	4. **Layer-2 attempt** — `ddx try <bead>` produces exactly one layer-2
844	   record, references its child layer-1 records, and finalizes the
845	   worktree as `merge` or `preserve`.
846	5. **Layer-3 drain** — `ddx work` produces exactly one layer-3 record
847	   per invocation, references its child layer-2 attempt ids, and reports
848	   one of the terminal dispositions (`drained`, `blocked`, `deferred`,
849	   `no_progress`, `signal`, `resource_exhausted`).
850	6. **Stop conditions** — `ddx work` evaluates the stop conditions
851	   between attempts and persists the evaluation log on the layer-3
852	   record.
853	7. **No-progress detection** — the `no_progress` condition is mechanical
854	   (no commit, no merged side-effect) and configurable via
855	   `.ddx/config.yaml` (default `N = 3`).
856	8. **Append-only history** — a new run never rewrites a previous record
857	   at any layer.
858	9. **Cross-layer query** — `ddx runs` lists records across all three
859	   layers with layer filtering; `ddx tries` and `ddx work workers`
860	   are layer-specific projections over the same substrate.
861	10. **Run detail inspection** — for any run id, callers can retrieve the
862	    common fields, the layer extension, attachments, and child run ids.
863	11. **Generated-artifact provenance** — runs that regenerate an artifact
864	    set `produces_artifact: <id>` on the run record; FEAT-007 reads this
865	    to populate `generated_by` graph edges.
866	12. **Migration from legacy paths** — readers tolerate
867	    `.ddx/exec-runs/` and `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` during a
868	    documented migration window and surface them as layer-1 / layer-2
869	    records; writers always target `.ddx/runs/`.
870	13. **Configuration** — substrate root, no-progress threshold, deferred
871	    budget, and retention settings are configurable in
872	    `.ddx/config.yaml`.
873	14. **Execution cleanup** — `ddx try` and `ddx work` remove stale DDx-owned
874	    execution worktrees, DDx-created test/e2e scratch roots, generated test
875	    binaries, and liveness files through inline, loop, and background cleanup
876	    paths without deleting preserved attempts or published evidence.
877	15. **Resource preflight** — `ddx try` validates writable execution roots and
878	    free bytes/inodes before claim; failed validation may trigger one cleanup
879	    retry, and `ddx work` / server-managed workers run cleanup before claim
880	    whenever temp free space drops below a soft high-water threshold. If the
881	    temp roots remain below the hard floor after cleanup, the loop returns
882	    `resource_exhausted` without claiming.
883	
884	Expected implementation tests include `TestExecutionCleanup_RemovesStaleDDXScratchDirs`,
885	`TestWorkResourcePreflight_RunsCleanupBelowSoftFloor`, and
886	`TestWorkResourcePreflight_StopsBelowHardFloorAfterCleanup`.
887	
888	### Non-Functional
889	
890	- **Determinism:** the persisted record reflects the exact prompt,
891	  response, logs, and structured result of one invocation.
892	- **Durability:** record writes are atomic or serialized so concurrent
893	  writers cannot leave partial records.
894	- **Observability:** stored records are human-readable enough for
895	  debugging and machine-parseable enough for automation.
896	- **Portability:** records remain repo-local and file-backed; no hosted
897	  service or database is required.
898	- **Low overhead:** layer-1 record bookkeeping adds minimal overhead
899	  beyond the underlying agent invocation.
900	- **Payload resilience:** large prompt, response, log, and bundle
901	  bodies are stored as attachments and never require rewriting a shared
902	  history file to persist one run safely.
903

936	
937	| MCP Tool | HTTP Endpoint | Description | Mode |
938	|----------|--------------|-------------|------|
939	| `ddx_runs_list` | `GET /api/runs` | List run records across layers, filterable by `layer`, `artifact`, `bead` | read |
940	| `ddx_runs_show` | `GET /api/runs/:id` | Show one run record with layer extension and attachment refs | read |
941	| `ddx_runs_log` | `GET /api/runs/:id/log` | Raw logs / attachment bodies | read |
942	| `ddx_runs_result` | `GET /api/runs/:id/result` | Structured result payload | read |
943	| `ddx_artifact_regenerate` | `POST /api/artifacts/:id/regenerate` | Trigger regeneration of one artifact; returns the new run id | **write — narrow** |
944	| (GraphQL) | `Mutation.runRequeue` | Re-queue the originating bead of an existing run record (manual operator re-queue from the Runs UI); requires `idempotencyKey`; emits a canonical `run_requeue` audit event on the bead (schema below) | **write — narrow** |
945	
946	The HTTP/MCP read surface is total over the unified run substrate.
947	
948	The write surfaces added by this feature are limited to
949	`artifactRegenerate` and `runRequeue`. All other run invocation
950	(layer-1 ad-hoc, layer-2 bead attempts, layer-3 queue drain) remains
951	CLI-only. Additional write endpoints require a separate feature update;
952	they are not implicit in this read-coverage expansion.
953	
954	### Re-queue audit event schema
955	
956	The `runRequeue` mutation reopens the originating bead and appends a
957	`run_requeue` event to that bead's audit log. Concurrent or repeat
958	submissions with the same `idempotencyKey` collapse to a single
959	re-queue and a single event (`deduplicated=true` is returned to all
960	subsequent callers); if the cached idempotency record points at a
961	missing bead, the requeue is replayed against the run's current
962	originating bead. The event uses the standard `bead.BeadEvent`
963	envelope and the following field schema:
964	
965	| Field | Value |
966	|---|---|
967	| `kind` | `run_requeue` (`RunRequeueEventKind`) |
968	| `summary` | `run requeued` |
969	| `actor` | Resolved operator identity; falls back to `anonymous` when no request identity is available |
970	| `source` | `graphql:runRequeue` |
971	| `body` | Single line `key=value` payload: `identity=<identity.kind> actor=<identity.actor> run_id=<runId> idempotency_key=<key> layer_override=<layer-or-empty>` |
972	
973	The body line is structured for grep/jq parsing: each token is a
974	`key=value` pair separated by single spaces. `layer_override` is the
975	empty string when the caller did not pass `RunRequeueInput.layer`.
976	`identity.kind` is the resolver-selected operator identity class
977	(`unknown`, `localhost`, or `tsnet`). `actor` is the resolved user or
978	operator label; when the request carries no identifying header, the
979	audit fields fall back to `identity=unknown actor=anonymous`.
980	
981	The GraphQL mutation response may also report `deduplicated=true`, but
982	that is a write-response flag, not part of the persisted audit event.
983	
984	Consumers of the audit log (FEAT-008 Runs row expansion, evaluation
985	skills under FEAT-019) treat `run_requeue` events as the canonical
986	record that a re-queue happened: there is no separate persisted
987	`requeue` record beyond (a) the new `open` status on the originating
988	bead and (b) this event entry.
989	
990	### Run-detail access audit events
991	
992	The first time the canonical project-scoped run detail is resolved for a
993	run record that is visible through project membership, DDx appends a
994	`run_detail_view` event to the originating bead's audit log. This is the
995	canonical record that a human inspected the run-detail surface.
996	
997	The event is read-side only: it is emitted on the canonical detail load,
998	not on in-page tab switches, evidence downloads, or breadcrumb/back
999	navigation. If the caller cannot see the project, there is no run detail
1000	and therefore no audit event.
1001	
1002	The `body` line is structured for grep/jq parsing and includes the
1003	project-scoped identity of the inspection:
1004	
1005	| Field | Value |
1006	|---|---|
1007	| `kind` | `run_detail_view` |
1008	| `summary` | `run detail viewed` |
1009	| `actor` | Viewer identity derived from the authenticated project member |
1010	| `source` | `graphql:run` |
1011	| `body` | Single line: `project_id=<projectId> run_id=<runId> layer=<layer> visibility=project_membership` |
1012	
1013	### Layer-to-substrate mapping for the Runs UI
1014	
1015	The web Runs view (FEAT-008 §5, FEAT-021) renders three layer chips
1016	backed by the unified substrate plus the legacy detail backings. The
1017	shared run-detail tab strip is the canonical drill-down surface used by
1018	FEAT-008, FEAT-019, and FEAT-021:
1019	
1020	| Chip | Substrate row | Detail backing for row expansion |
1021	|---|---|---|
1022	| `work` | `layer: 3` Run record | Layer-3 record's queue inputs / stop-condition log / child layer-2 attempt ids; the shared tab strip exposes `overview` only |
1023	| `try` | `layer: 2` Run record | The `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` bundle attached to the layer-2 record (manifest, prompt, result, checks, verdict); the shared tab strip exposes `overview`, `prompt`, `response`, `tools`, and `evidence` |
1024	| `run` | `layer: 1` Run record | The associated `AgentSession` row (prompt / response / stderr / billing / cached-token detail) joined onto the layer-1 record; the shared tab strip exposes `overview`, `prompt`, `response`, `session`, `tools`, and `evidence` |
1025	
1026	`AgentSession` rows that have no parent layer-2 attempt (raw `ddx
1027	agent log` invocations) surface as synthesized `layer=run` Runs rows
1028	keyed by session id, so no agent-session row is dropped during the
1029	Sessions/Executions tab retirement.
1030	
1031	The shared tab strip is:
1032	
1033	- `work` → `overview`
1034	- `try` → `overview`, `prompt`, `response`, `tools`, `evidence`
1035	- `run` → `overview`, `prompt`, `response`, `session`, `tools`, `evidence`
1036	
1037	## User Stories
1038	
1039	### US-090: Developer Inspects a Layer-1 Run
1040	
1041	**As a** developer running an ad-hoc agent invocation
1042	**I want** to inspect the resulting run record
1043	**So that** I can audit prompt, response, tokens, and side-effects
1044	
1045	**Acceptance Criteria:**
1046	- Given I invoke `ddx run`, then DDx writes one layer-1 record under
1047	  `.ddx/runs/<run-id>/` with `layer: 1` and the layer-1 extension
1048	  populated.
1049	- Given the run completes, when I run `ddx runs show <run-id>`, then I
1050	  see common fields, the layer-1 extension, requested `MinPower`/`MaxPower`,
1051	  actual model/power, requested passthrough constraints if supplied, and attachment
1052	  references.
1053	
1054	### US-091: Developer Inspects a Layer-2 Bead Attempt
1055	
1056	**As a** developer reviewing a bead attempt
1057	**I want** to see the attempt's worktree finalization and the child
1058	layer-1 invocations
1059	**So that** I can audit what the agent did inside the attempt
1060	
1061	**Acceptance Criteria:**
1062	- Given I invoke `ddx try <bead-id>`, then DDx writes one layer-2
1063	  record with `layer: 2` referencing one or more layer-1 run ids.
1064	- Given the attempt finalizes, when I run `ddx runs show <run-id>`,
1065	  then the record reports `merge` or `preserve` and surfaces the
1066	  evidence bundle.
1067	
1068	### US-092: Developer Drains the Queue and Audits the Drain
1069	
1070	**As a** developer running `ddx work`
1071	**I want** the loop to stop on a clearly named condition
1072	**So that** I can tell whether the queue was drained, blocked, deferred,
1073	spinning, or interrupted
1074	
1075	**Acceptance Criteria:**
1076	- Given I invoke `ddx work`, then DDx writes exactly one layer-3 record
1077	  per invocation referencing each child layer-2 attempt id.
1078	- Given I press Ctrl-C while `ddx work` or `ddx try` is running, then DDx
1079	  prints `Cancel received, shutting down gracefully` before cleanup begins.
1080	- Given the loop stops, then the layer-3 record's terminal disposition
1081	  is one of `drained`, `blocked`, `deferred`, `no_progress`, `signal`,
1082	  and the evaluation log names which condition fired and on which
1083	  iteration.
1084	
1085	### US-093: No-Progress Detection Stops a Spinning Loop
1086	
1087	**As a** developer running `ddx work`
1088	**I want** the loop to stop when consecutive attempts make no progress
1089	**So that** I do not burn budget on a pathological queue state
1090	
1091	**Acceptance Criteria:**
1092	- Given `N` consecutive valid implementation attempts produce no commit and no
1093	  merged side-effect (default `N = 3`, configurable), then the next
1094	  stop-condition evaluation triggers `no_progress` and the layer-3 record
1095	  terminates with that disposition.
1096	- Given a candidate is skipped by readiness, claim race, routing preflight,
1097	  quota/transport/auth/tool setup failure, review error, or operator-action
1098	  class, then that iteration does not increment the no-progress counter.
1099	- Given retry policy permits escalation before `no_progress` fires, when
1100	  a retry is scheduled, then the next layer-1 run raises `MinPower`
1101	  and records the actual model/power returned by the agent.
1102	- Given the original invocation supplied `--harness`, `--provider`, or
1103	  `--model`, then retries pass the same values through unchanged and DDx does
1104	  not inspect those values when choosing the next requested `MinPower`.
1105	- Given retry escalation would exceed the power available under hard
1106	  passthrough pins, then DDx stops with `blocked_by_passthrough_constraint` or
1107	  `agent_power_unsatisfied`, records the Fizeau-supplied evidence, and does not
1108	  mutate the pins or call `ResolveRoute`.
1109	
1110	### US-094: Cross-Layer Run History
1111	
1112	**As a** workflow tool
1113	**I want** to query run history across the task execution layers from one place
1114	**So that** I do not have to merge two on-disk shapes by hand
1115	
1116	**Acceptance Criteria:**
1117	- Given `run`, `try`, and `work` records exist, when a tool calls
1118	  `GET /api/runs?layer=...`, then it receives records of the requested
1119	  layer with consistent common fields.
1120	- Given a layer-3 record exists, then its child layer-2 attempt ids and
1121	  their child layer-1 run ids are reachable by following
1122	  `parent_run_id` pointers.
1123	
1124	### US-095: Artifact Regeneration Records Provenance
1125	
1126	**As a** consumer of the document graph
1127	**I want** generated artifacts to point back to the run that produced
1128	them
1129	**So that** staleness can be evaluated against generator inputs
1130	
1131	**Acceptance Criteria:**
1132	- Given I regenerate an artifact via `ddx artifact regenerate <id>`,
1133	  then the resulting run record has `produces_artifact: <id>`.
1134	- Given a generated artifact has been produced by a run, then
1135	  FEAT-007's `generated_by` edge resolves to that run id.
1136	
1137	### US-096: Operator Migrates From Legacy Run Trees
1138	
1139	**As a** repo operator adopting the unified substrate
1140	**I want** legacy `.ddx/exec-runs/` and `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/`
1141	records to remain inspectable during the migration window
1142	**So that** adopting `.ddx/runs/` does not strand prior evidence
1143	
1144	**Acceptance Criteria:**
1145	- Given a repository contains legacy run data, when DDx reads run
1146	  history, then legacy paths surface through the unified read API as
1147	  layer-1 / layer-2 records during the documented migration window.
1148	- Given the migration window closes, then writers target only
1149	  `.ddx/runs/` and a one-shot rewrite has normalized prior records into
1150	  the unified substrate.
1151	
1152	## CONTRACT-003 Amendment
1153	
1154	This feature requires two narrow read-only-amendment-adjacent changes
1155	to CONTRACT-003: write surfaces for `artifactRegenerate` (HTTP/MCP) and
1156	`runRequeue` (GraphQL). `runRequeue` does not invoke the execution service —
1157	it reopens the originating bead so the existing `ddx work` loop can claim it
1158	again — but it is still a write because it mutates bead state and appends an
1159	audit event (see "Re-queue audit event schema" above).
1160	No other write surfaces are added; in particular, layer-1, layer-2,
1161	and layer-3 invocation remain CLI-only.
1162	
1163	If FEAT-006 implementation reveals additional CONTRACT-003 gaps (e.g.,
1164	upstream Cobra root export details), those amendments are scoped to
1165	FEAT-006 and not to this feature.
1166	
1167	## Dependencies
1168	
1169	- FEAT-005 (Artifacts) — `produces_artifact` references artifact IDs
1170	- FEAT-006 (Fizeau consumer contract) — provides the layer-1 consumer-side wrapper
1171	  and CONTRACT-003 boundary
1172	- FEAT-007 (Doc Graph) — `generated_by` edges read `produces_artifact`
1173	  from run records
1174	- DDx CLI infrastructure (config loading, command factory)
1175	
1176	## Out of Scope
1177	
1178	- **Run kinds beyond `run`, `try`, and `work`** — comparison, replay, benchmark,
1179	  adversarial review, effort estimate, and similar workflows are skill
1180	  compositions, not new layers or new on-disk shapes.
1181	- **Content-aware supervisory loop decisions** — `ddx work` performs
1182	  mechanical queue drain only. Decisions like "comparison failed →
1183	  enqueue reconciliation beads" are skill / plugin / workflow-tool
1184	  territory.
1185	- **Server-side layer-1/2/3 invocation** — only `artifactRegenerate`
1186	  has a write endpoint in this feature.
1187	- **Workflow-specific action semantics** — phase routing, methodology
1188	  scoring, tool-specific issue closing — delegated to workflow tools.
1189	- **Workflow-specific autonomy semantics** — DDx owns the mechanical retry,
1190	  review, cooldown, and budget policy in this feature and ADR-024. Higher-level
1191	  methodology decisions such as "file reconciliation beads after a benchmark"
1192	  remain workflow-tool territory.
1193	- **Hosted run history storage** — records are repo-local and
1194	  file-backed.
1195	- **Separate domain-specific runtime stores** — there is one substrate.
